coredata.dc:title: Chapter 5 Nanomaterials-based UV photodetectors
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2019 Elsevier Inc. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Nasiri, Noushin
Tricoli, Antonio
coredata.dc:description: In this chapter, we provide a comprehensive summary on the latest achievements in the design and fabrication of ultraviolet visible-blind photodetectors based on the nanostructured materials, and of some of their exciting applications. We commence by summarizing the fundamental performance indicators and figures of merit of photodetectors. We discuss the most common design layouts and their photodetection mechanisms. A detailed review of the utilization of zero-, one-, two-, and three-dimensional materials for the fabrication of advanced photodetectors is provided with focus on the most successful materials engineering strategies. The benefits of the various materials and designs are critically examined providing directions for future improvement and possible research directions. We conclude with a broad appraisal of the latest achievements and of the key milestones toward the development of the future generation of ultra-miniaturized, very low-power ultra-violet photodetectors.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photodetectors
Nanomaterials
Ultraviolet
One-dimensional
Two-dimensional
Three-dimensional

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation and current-voltage characteristic of (A, B) Ohmic contact photoresistor (C, D) Schottky contact, and (E, F) photodiodes.

ce:figure.ce:label: Fig. 2

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of a (A, C) nanowire [2] and a (B, D) nanoparticles network [4] photoconductor under (A, B) dark condition and (C, D) UV illumination. In dark condition (A, B), oxygen molecules adsorb on the surface and capture free electron (blue spheres) present in the n-type semiconductor forming a low-conductivity depletion layer near the surface. Under UV illumination (C, D), the photogenerated holes (gold spheres) migrate to the surface and are trapped, leaving unpaired electrons in the nanowire that contribute to the photocurrent. The lifetime of the unpaired electrons is further increased by oxygen molecules desorption from the surface when holes neutralize the oxygen ions.

ce:figure.ce:source: Reproduced with permission [2,4]. Copyright 2007, ACS Publications, Copyright 2015, Wiley Online Library.

ce:figure.ce:label: Fig. 3

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Schematic illustration of the configuration for photoconductive measurements of GQDs with asymmetric Ag-Au electrodes. Inset: Cross-sectional SEM image of the GQD layer on silicon substrate. (B) Time response behavior of the device under pulsed 254 and 302nm DUV light with 42μWcm−2 light intensity and 5V applied bias. (C) I-V characteristics of the device measured in the dark and under continuous 254 and 302nm DUV light [52]. (D) Schematic of the fabricated photodetector illustrating the one-dimensional CQD array geometry. Inset: a zoom-in of the nanogap area. (E) Room-temperature I-V characteristics before and after deposition of PbSe CQDs in vacuum. (F) Photocurrent response (bottom panel) of the device to a 1μs square pulsed laser illumination (to panel, λ =670nm, E =0.85Wcm−2, spot size=150mm). The sire and fall time of the laser signal is <20s [55].

ce:figure.ce:source: Reproduced with permission [52,55]. Copyright 2015, Copyright 2012, ACS Publications.

ce:figure.ce:label: Fig. 5

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Schematic illustration of the SiNW array/CQD heterojunction device. (B) SEM images of the CH3-terminated SiNW arrays coated with CQDs. (C, D) TEM images of individual SiNWs coated with CQDs. Insets in (C) are the EDS analysis on the CQD-coated SiNWs. (E) Photovoltaic characteristics, and (F) EQE spectra of the optimal SiNW array/CQD heterojunction device and SiNW-array-based device without coating of CQDs as well as the CQD/planar Si heterojunction device for comparison. (G) Energy band diagrams of the Au/SiNW array Schottky junction (left) and the SiNW array/CQD heterojunction (right) [58]. (H) Schematics of carrier separation mechanism in the ZnO QD decorated ZTO NW photodetector upon UV illumination. (I) Spectral responsivity of photodetectors made of a pristine ZTO NW and a ZnO QD decorated ZTO NW at different wavelengths ranging from 250 to 900nm at a bias voltage of 1V. The inset is the schematic illustration of the as-fabricated photodetector on a SiO2/Si substrate. (J) Simulated I-V curves of two kinds of photodetectors under dark and 300nm UV illumination with a light intensity of 0.67μWcm−2 [59].

ce:figure.ce:source: Reproduced with permission [58,59]. Copyright 2014, Copyright 2017, ACS Publications.

ce:figure.ce:label: Fig. 7

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of (A) top and (B) cross-sectional view of the single-layer MoS2 photodetector. (C) Optical image of the device consists of a field-effect transistor with two gold contacts and gated using a degenerately doped silicon substrate. Scale bar, 10 mm. (D) Band diagram of the monolayer MoS2 photodetector taking into consideration small Schottky barriers at the contacts. (E) Photoresponsivity of a similar monolayer MoS2 device as a function of illumination wavelength from 400 to 800 nm. Inset: Drain-source (I ds−V ds) characteristic of the device in the dark and under different illumination intensities [69]. (F) Fabrication process schematic of Gr−WS2−Gr photodetector array. (G) Photoresponsivity of the four types of detectors under an incident light density of 3.8 × 106 mW cm− 2 [71].

ce:figure.ce:source: Reproduced with permission [69,71]. Copyright 2013, Nature Publishing Group, Copyright 2016, ACS Publications.

ce:figure.ce:label: Fig. 9

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: 45° view SEM micrographs of (A) the ZnO/Si branched nanowire heterostructures at low magnification and (B) a photodetector device with top ITO contact. (C) I-V characteristics measured in the dark and under xenon lamp illumination [82]. Structural characterization of a ZnO/ZnS core/shell nanowire showing low magnification top view, FESEM images of (D) a ZnO/ZnS core/shell nanowire array and (E) an HRTEM image of a single ZnO/ZnS core/shell nanowire. (F) Electrical characterization of ZnO/ZnS core/shell nanowire array devices showing the photocurrent response under dark, green, blue, and UV excitation [83]. SEM images of (G) CH3NH3PbI3 perovskite thin-film surface with island-structured morphology and (H) cross-sectional structure of the representative device. (I) Transient photoresponse properties with bias voltage of 5, 15, 30, and 60V under a power density of 6.59mWcm−2 [85].

ce:figure.ce:source: Reproduced with permission [82,83,85]. Copyright 2010, Copyright 2015, ACS Publications.

ce:figure.ce:label: Fig. 10

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) SEM image of an exemplary ZnO nanoparticle networks deposited for 100s and composed of (B) ZnO nanoparticles with average particle diameter of 18nm [9]. (C) Spectral responsivity of the ultraporous nanostructured photodetectors as a function of the annealing temperature and illumination wavelengths. (D) Dark- and photocurrent of the ultraporous nanostructured device annealed at the optimal temperature of 300°C [8]. (E) SEM image of the resulting NiO/ZnO morphology with 40 s NiO sputtering time. (F) TEM images of the NiO/ZnO grains removed from the films [9]. (G) I-V characteristics of the pure ZnO and NiO/ZnO networks as a function of the NiO sputtering time, at illumination wavelength of 370nm and applied bias of 1V. (H) Normalized photocurrent dynamics of the pure ZnO and NiO/ZnO networks as a function of the NiO sputtering time [9].

ce:figure.ce:source: Reproduced with permission [8,9]. Copyright 2016, Copyright 2017, Wiley Online Library.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Schematic diagram of CQD photoconductive photodetector and photograph of fabricated device on stone paper. Flexibility of paper-based PbS CQD photodetector. (B) Dynamic current-time curves of PbS CQDs/Au NCs nanocomposites photodetector when bent with different curvatures and (C) device performance after many cycles of bending and unbending. Insets showed photographs of devices being bent. All devices were measured at 100 V bias, 650 nm illumination, and 430 mW cm− 2 [57].

ce:figure.ce:label: Fig. 6

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) SEM image of a diameter-modulated Ge NW. Inset: Magnified SEM views and high-resolution TEM images at each end of the NW. (B) Schematics of the scanning photocurrent measurement setup for the conical Ge NW multiterminal device. Scanning photocurrent images (color) by a lock-in current measurement at V = 0.1 V overlaid on reflection images (gray) of the device. Insets: the NW end segments of different diameters under investigation. (C) Photoconductivity as a function of absorbed power density with different diameters of 50, 60, 150, and 290 nm at V = 0.5 V [61].

ce:figure.ce:source: Reproduced with permission [61]. Copyright 2010, ACS Publications.

ce:figure.ce:label: Fig. 8

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Schematics of the device structure: few-layer black phosphorus flakes were exfoliated onto monolayer MoS2 in order to form a van der Waals heterojunction. (B) Optical image of the p-n heterojunction used for photocurrent mapping. Scale bar, 5 μm. (C) Photocurrent mapping of the p-n heterojunction under 0 V bias. The dashed and solid lines outline the heterojunction regions and the metal contacts, respectively. Contacts A and B are electrically connected to black phosphorus and MoS2, respectively. (D) Gate tunable I-V characteristics of the 2D p-n diode. The current increases as the back gate voltage increases [74].

ce:figure.ce:source: Reproduced with permission [74]. Copyright 2014, ACS Publications.

ce:figure.ce:label: Fig. 11

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Schematic and band diagram of a band-selective UV photodetector, composed of a photoresistor ZnO bottom layer, a very thin amorphous SiO2 space layer, and an anatase TiO2 long-pass filter top layer. (B) Normalized responsivity spectra of the fabricated devices as a function of TiO2 film thickness. (C) The photoresponse of the device with a 4.4-μm thick TiO2 layer to the UVA, UVB, and visible light, as a function of the light density [5].

ce:figure.ce:source: Reproduced with permission [5]. Copyright 2016, Wiley Online Library.

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Engineering, Faculty of Science and Engineering, Macquarie University, Sydney, NSW, Australia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Engineering, Faculty of Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nanotechnology Research Laboratory, Research School of Engineering, College of Engineering and Computer Sciences, Australian National University, Canberra, ACT, Australia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Nanotechnology Research Laboratory, Research School of Engineering, College of Engineering and Computer Sciences

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this chapter, we provide a comprehensive summary on the latest achievements in the design and fabrication of ultraviolet visible-blind photodetectors based on the nanostructured materials, and of some of their exciting applications. We commence by summarizing the fundamental performance indicators and figures of merit of photodetectors. We discuss the most common design layouts and their photodetection mechanisms. A detailed review of the utilization of zero-, one-, two-, and three-dimensional materials for the fabrication of advanced photodetectors is provided with focus on the most successful materials engineering strategies. The benefits of the various materials and designs are critically examined providing directions for future improvement and possible research directions. We conclude with a broad appraisal of the latest achievements and of the key milestones toward the development of the future generation of ultra-miniaturized, very low-power ultra-violet photodetectors.

ce:section.ce:para: N.N. thankfully acknowledges the support of AMP Services Limited. A.T. gratefully acknowledges the support of Australian Research Council DP150101939, Australian Research Council DE160100569 and Westpac2016 Research Fellowship.
The authors have no conflict of interest to declare.

Photodetectors are essential elements applied in video imaging, optical communications, biomedical imaging, security, night-vision, gas sensing, and motion detection, which possess the ability to transform light into electrical signals precisely. As the scale and diversity of application areas are growing, the need for innovative photodetection platform technologies with higher performance in terms of speed, efficiency or wavelength range, as well as material flexibility, transparency, and complementary metal-oxide-semiconductor (CMOS) integrability, is becoming more critical. In the past decades, extensive efforts have been devoted to explore the next generation of photodetector materials, such as In2Te3, ZnO, and GaN, with low noise, high photosensitivity, and good stability. However, many of these novel photodetector materials still suffer from limited photocurrent and photoresponse speed.

Inspired by nanoscience and nanoengineering, numerous nanostructured materials have been recently developed exhibiting excellent photoelectronic properties. As a new class of building block, nanoscale elements in terms of quantum dots (QDs), nanowires, and nanolayers can be used for fabricating high-performance photodetectors. They often exhibit an appealing performance for numerous practical applications including high miniaturization potential, high sensitivity, fast response, and multifunctionality. Here, we discuss the recent advances in low-dimensional nanostructured materials for photodetectors. We provide a critical assessment of the key figures of merits of state-of-the-art nanostructured layouts highlighting structural-functional correlations and critical features of the most performing devices. We will focus on the impact of the nano- to microscale material hierarchy discussing commonalities and discrepancies across materials and morphologies.

Wide bandgap semiconductors have been investigated in many recent studies to understand the fundamental process and enhancing the performance of visible-blind UV light photodetectors [1–5]. Such photodetectors rely on the transduction of absorbed photons into electrical signals [6,7]. The UV photoresponse characteristics of nanostructured devices are influenced by a number of factors, such as the presence of different amounts of defects [8,9], crystallographic orientation [10,11], grain size [7,8], and processing condition [12]. These factors significantly influence the photoresponse behaviors explaining the large variation in the performance observed by various groups for similar materials [4,9,13]. Herein, we focus on three types of device configurations, providing guidelines and structural-functional correlations for nanostructured UV photodetector devices.

A photoconductor, consisting of a semiconductor with two ohmic contacts, is essentially a radiation-sensitive resistor. The schematic structure and operation of such photoconductor layouts are shown in Fig. 1 A and B. The most common UV photoresistor is ZnO, which has been investigated in detail by a number of groups resulting in an excellent photodetection performance [4,14–16]. The high performance of ZnO is attributed to oxygen-related hole-trap states at the photodetector surface [15,17]. In short, surface traps tend to adsorb oxygen molecules from the air causing capture of free electrons from the n-type semiconductor (Fig. 2 A and B) [4]. This leads to the formation of an electron-depleted region near the surface and band bending, which significantly decreases the device’s conductance (Fig. 2B) [4]. The surface band bending establishes an internal electric field, which spatially separates photogenerated electron-holes leading to suppressed photocarrier recombination and dramatically prolonging carrier lifetimes [8,9,18]. These effects are particularly prominent in nanocrystalline films, where the surface area is large and the depletion regions may extend throughout the entire film [4,7–9].

Upon illumination with photon energy higher or equal to the semiconductor band gap, electron-hole pairs are generated [4,19]. In fact, the electrons in the valence band (VB) are excited to the conduction band (CB) with simultaneous generation of the same number of holes in the VB (Fig. 2C and D) [2]. The holes that migrate to the surface along the potential gradient produced by band bending either discharge the negatively charged adsorbed oxygen ions to photodesorb oxygen from the surfaces or effectively get trapped at the ZnO surfaces (Fig. 2C and D) [2,4]. This results in an increase in the free-carrier concentration and a decrease in the width of the depletion layer (Fig. 2C and D). When the UV illumination is switched off, the residual accumulated holes recombine with the unpaired electrons, while oxygen is gradually reabsorbed into the surface resulting in a slow current decay [2,4].

Schottky diodes in their simplest form consist of a metal layer, such as gold, silver, and aluminum, in contact with a moderately doped n-type semiconductor [20]. Fig. 1C and D presents the schematic structure of the Schottky contact photodetector. It is a unipolar device because it has electrons as majority carriers on both sides of the junction [21]. Hence, there is no depletion layer formed near the junction and no significant current from the metal to the semiconductor with reverse bias. Thus, the delay present in the junction diodes due to hole-electron recombination time is absent here [20]. Due to the electrostatic barrier between the metal and the semiconductor caused by the difference in their work functions, the Schottky contact devices exhibit a rectifying behavior [22].

Two mechanisms contribute to the photoresponse of these devices. The first is the “primary current” maintained by carriers optically generated in the depletion region. The “secondary current” is due to the lowering of the Schottky barrier caused by illumination and trapping at interface states [20,23]. In fact, in the Schottky contact devices, the photogenerated electron-hole pairs can be separated quickly by the local electric field at the Schottky barrier region, which reduces the electron-hole recombination rates and increases the carrier lifetime, resulting in an increase in the free-carrier density [24,25]. The increased carrier density in the semiconductor photodetector changes the Fermi energy and the work function of semiconductor, resulting in the reduction of the Schottky barrier height and width between the semiconductor layer and electrodes [20,24,25]. Both of these account for the high photocurrent on-off ratio and rapid response time of the UV detectors based on the Schottky-type configurations. Once the UV illumination is turned off, electron-hole pairs will quickly recombine in the semiconductors, which may explain the fast decay times in these types of UV photodetectors [26,27].

A photodiode is a p-n junction or p-i-n structure, which is commonly used in a variety of electronic applications, including UV photodetection [28]. The device consists of a highly doped transparent p-type layer and an n-type highly doped layer (Fig. 1E) with an undoped absorbing layer in the middle in the case of p-i-n structure. When p- and n-type materials are placed in contact with each other, the junction behaves very differently than either type of material alone [29] (Fig. 1F). Specifically, current will flow readily in one direction (forward biased) but not in the other (reverse biased), achieving the basic diode function (Fig. 1F). This nonreversing behavior arises from the nature of the charge transport process in the two types of materials [28,29]. In the p-type region, there are holes from the acceptor impurities and in the n-type region, there are extra electrons. When a p-n junction is formed, some of the electrons from the n-region, which have reached the CB, are free to diffuse across the junction and combine with holes. Filling a hole makes a negative ion and leaves behind a positive ion on the n-side. A space charge builds up, creating a depletion region, which inhibits any further electron transfer unless it is helped by putting a forward bias on the junction. Under UV illumination, the photogenerated electron-hole pairs contribute to an induced photocurrent with a large current transport under reverse bias [28,29].

Photodetection is usually referred as the process of converting light signals to electric signals including three physical mechanisms of light harvesting, exciton separation, and charge carrier transport to corresponding electrodes. To quantify and compare the performance of different types of photodetectors, it is essential to have an in-depth understanding of their figures of merit. In the past decades, a number of different figures of merit have been introduced in order to characterize UV photodetectors. Here, we discuss the most common figure of merits utilized by the research community.

By definition, responsivity is the ratio of photocurrent obtained by a device per unit area to the incident light power at a given wavelength and can be calculated as follows [5,8,9]:
(1)
R =
I ph
P opt
where I ph is the output current obtained by the device per unit area and P opt is the illumination power on the detection area of the device. The spectral response of the photodetector is obtained by plotting the responsivity against the wavelength, and usually follows the absorption spectrum of the photosensitive semiconductor material used for the fabrication of the detector [4,7].

Another important figure of merit is the detectivity, which represents the ability to differentiate the smallest light intensity from noise. It is defined as [8,9]
(2)
D =
AB
NEP
where A is the effective area of the detector, B is the bandwidth, and NEP is the noise-equivalent power. If the dark current is the major contribution to the noise, this equation can be simplified to [8,9,30]
(3)
D =
R
2 e .
I dark
A
1 2
where R is the responsivity, e is the electron charge (1.6×10−19 coulombs), and I dark is the dark current of the device.

By definition, photoconductive gain (G) is defined as the ratio of the number of electrons collected per unit time (N electron) to the number of absorbed photons per unit time (N photon) which can be derived by [2,18,31]
(4)
G =
N electron
N photon
=
I photo
−
I dark
e
hv P
1 η
where I photo is the photo current, I dark is the dark current, e is the electron charge (1.6×10−19 coulombs), hv is the photon energy of incident light (5.37×10−19 J for 370nm), P is the power of incident light, and η is the quantum efficiency.

The efficiency of light energy utilization can be described by external quantum efficiency (EQE) [32,33]
(5)
EQE =
I photo
e Ф
=
hv e
I photo
P
where I photo is the photo current, e is the electron charge (1.6×10−19 coulombs), Ф is the photon flux, defined as the number of incident photons, hv is the energy of one single photon, and P is the power of incident light.

The linear dynamic range (LDR) of a detector is a measurement of the minimum detectable signal, to the maximum signal that will start to compress the detector. LDR is one of the important figures of merit for a photodetector which is defined as the maximum linear response of the device divided by the detector noise. Most detectors eventually become nonlinear as sample size is increased and this upper point is usually well defined. The LDR can be calculated as [34,35]
(6)
LDR = 20 log
I photo
I dark
where I photo is the photocurrent and I dark is the dark current.

Nanostructured materials and their systematic arrangement in well-defined nano-microscale configurations are leading the development of novel devices with unprecedented performance and functionalities. In particular, the novel capabilities to control the dimensionalities of such nanostructured components [36–38] enable to take advantage of atomic-scale phenomena transducing them in a multitude of signals. Tuning the electronic density of states and band structure of the material is also particularly effective at the nanoscale making it possible to tailor the material properties optimally for the intended applications [39,40]. Usually, nanomaterial is categorized in zero-dimensional (0D), one-dimensional (1D), two-dimensional (2D), and three-dimensional (3D) as a function of their confinement in the nanoscale. In this subchapter, some exemplary achievements in the design of nanomaterials for UV photodetectors are summarized.

0D nanomaterials are defined as materials that are limited in the scale of few nanometers in all dimensions. These include QDs and are attracting significant attention as multifunctional building blocks for future electronic and optoelectronic devices [41,42]. Recently, numerous nanofabrication methods have been reported for the design of well-defined 0D nanomaterials providing superior control over morphologies, crystallinity, and composition [43,44]. UV photodetectors have been successfully fabricated using a various range of 0D nanomaterials such as QDs, heterogeneous particles arrays [45], core-shell QDs [46], and nanolenses [47].

For instance, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have recently attracted much scientific interest thanks to the low cost and abundancy of carbon as well as its unique physical properties [48,49]. They usually consist of very thin (3–20nm) graphene sheets that exhibit exciton confinement and quantum-size effect. Notably, the infinite exciton Bohr radius of graphene allows quantum confinement in finite-sized structures overcoming partially some of its zero band-gap limitations for light detection [50,51]. More in details, graphene absorbs light weekly over a broad wavelength range resulting in no spectral selectivity and low photo-responsivity [48]. It was recently reported [52] that confining graphene in 0D QDs allows the fabrication of deep UV (DUV) photodetectors down to wavelength of 254 nm. Fig. 3 A illustrates the configuration of such a GQDs photodetectors that use an asymmetric Au-Ag electrode layout. As a comparison, symmetric Au-Au and Ag-Ag electrode pair were investigated. An aqueous solution of GQDs was drop casted on the electrode pairs, previously fabricated on a quartz substrate, and thereafter dried at 70°C for 15min [52]. The GQD layer has a thickness of ca. 80nm (Fig. 3A, inset) and an irregular, uneven morphology. The latter is usually the result of the evaporation of the solvents and the strong capillary forces acting on the nanomaterial in wet-synthesis processes.

It was shown that the asymmetric or symmetric device architecture had no significant effect on the dark current of the device (Fig. 3B and C). However, in terms of photocurrent, the asymmetric devices have a rectifying behavior not observed for the symmetric layout. The former achieved a rectification ratio of about 20 at ±5V under 254nm light illumination [52]. Also, the photocurrent was significantly improved in comparison to the devices having a symmetric electrode layout [52]. An increase of the photocurrent from 71.3 to 8823pA was observed by decreasing the illumination wavelength from under 302 to 254nm. The resulting I photo/I dark current ratios of the asymmetric Ag-Au electrode photodetectors were 48 and 5882 for 302 and 254nm light illumination with a bias of 5V, respectively. In comparison, the symmetric Au-Au electrode devices achieved a I photo/I dark ratios of only 1.2 and 2 for illumination at 302–254nm, respectively [52].

Developing devices with high photoconductive gain is an important advancement in the field of quantum-dot photodetectors, where exciton generation in the device is followed by trapping of one of the charge carriers, thereby lowering the chemical potential for transport of the opposite charge carrier. If the trap lifetime surpasses the transit time of the opposite charge carrier, the device exhibits a high photoconductance which is mainly attributed to the number of carriers passing through the circuit before recombination occurs [53]. However, this results in a longer response time (in range of milliseconds) which is not fast for many applications including video-rate laser-scanning microscopy [42,54]. To overcome this limitation, Prins et al. [55] fabricated colloidal QDs-based photodetector (Fig. 3D) featuring an EQE of 38 electrons per photon while exhibiting a response time of faster than 300ns. Using dip-coating technique, they placed a 1D (4nm in size) parallel array of PbSe QDs in direct contact with nanometer-separated electrodes with an electrode separation of 4m and width of 10μm (Fig. 3D) [55]. Unlike conventional QD thin-film photodetectors, charge mobility no longer plays a role in this carbon QD (CQD)-junction as the contact of both source and drain electrodes to each CQD allows for direct charge extraction resulting in a higher photodetection performance as well as faster response [55].

I-V characteristics of the fabricated device under laser light irradiation with the wavelength of 532nm and light density of 0.16Wcm−2 is presented in Fig. 3E, demonstrating a linear dependence with the applied voltage in the low-bias regime [55] Unlike the conventional QD thin-film photodetectors, charge mobility no longer plays a role in this CQD-junction as the contact of both source and drain electrodes to each CQD enables the direct charge extraction resulting in the combination of high efficiency with fast response by the device. Fig. 3F shows a high photoconductive response (consistently above 2nA) exhibited by the fabricated device when the laser spot places directly on top of the nanogap area [55]. In addition to the high efficiency of the presented architecture, the device featured a fast rise and fall time of approximately 200 and 300ns to a 1μs laser pulse [55]. Such fast response times combined with high efficiencies are crucial parameters in many applications including video-rate laser-scanning microscopy where dwell times in the order of 100ns are required [56].

In a similar approach, He et al. [57] fabricated a photoconductive device composed of PbS CQD film sandwiched synthesized using hot-injection method, deposited between two graphite electrodes deposited on a stone paper as substrate (Fig. 4 A). The Au nanocrystals (NCs) were later incorporated into PbS CQD film to serve as the relay for hole transport and reduce the carrier transit time, resulted in enhanced detectivity of the fabricated device [57]. In order to investigate the device potential application for flexible optoelectronics, the bending and fatigue properties of the fabricated PbS CQD photodetectors were examined under a bending curvature of 0–80degrees (Fig. 4B) [57]. No significant change was observed for the device temporal response under a bending curvature of 80degrees while the photocurrent slightly reduced compared to the unbended condition. The photoresponse performance of the device was further examined after several bending cycles (at 20degrees) resulted in less than 10% decay in photocurrent (Fig. 4C), indicating excellent flexibility robustness of PbS CQD photodetectors built by this technique [57].

1D nanomaterials are usually defined as materials having a high aspect ratio between one dimension, extending in the range of hundreds of nanometers to several micrometers, and other two dimensions. Ordered with increasing aspect ratio, they are usually described as nanorods, nanowires, and nanofibers. A major advantage is the possibility of optimizing individually the optical thickness and the photogenerated electron-hole collection path. Recently, it was shown that the combination of 1D Si nanowires arrays with CQDs (Fig. 5 A) led to silicon nanowire (SiNW) array/CQD core-shell heterojunction with superior performance. Fig. 5B shows images of NW with an average length and diameter of 2μm and hundreds of nanometers, respectively. Notably, transmission electron microscopy (TEM) analysis of the nanowires (Fig. 5C,D) shows that a full encapsulation of the SiNW by a continuous CQD shell is achieved with an approximate thickness of 23nm [58]. These hybrid 1D/0D structure utilized in photovoltaic devices is able to suppress recombination, while enhancing optical absorption. The CQD coating effectively function as an electron blocking layer decreasing the recombination in the SiNW array/CQD devices [58]. Fig. 5G shows the charge separation and transport mechanism in such SiNW array/CQD heterojunction solar cells. Shortly, under light irradiation, the generated holes in the Si diffuse to the SiNW/CQD interface where they are effectively separated by the photogenerated electron the built-in electric field of the heterojunction. The photo-excited electrons in the CB of Si migrate and are transferred to the bottom In-Ga electrode. The injection of the photo-excited electrons from the SiNW to the top Au electrode is prevented by the CQD coating [58]. At the same time, the photogenerated holes can be transferred to the CQDs coatings and then transferred to the Au electrode, resulted in a rectifying behavior and the enhancement of the photocurrent [58]. As a comparative layout, the energy band diagram of the non-CQDs-coated Au/SiNW array Schottky junction is also depicted in Fig. 5G. Without the CQD electron blocking layer, electrons may diffuse toward the Au and recombine, resulting in a large J s and therefore, a low V oc of the solar cells (Fig. 5E). Notably, this hybrid SiNW array/CQD core-shell heterojunction device exhibited a large EQE of above 60% over a wide range of wavelengths from 400 to 950nm, while the planar Si/CQD layout resulted in EQE below 50% (Fig. 5F) [58]. In addition to the excellent light harvesting properties, this nanomaterial composition and layout demonstrated also a high sensitivity as a photodetector to wavelength of 600nm. They achieved a high photosensitivity of 3×10 [3], large responsivity of 353mAW−1, and fast response speed of 20–40μs under 100mWcm−2 and zero bias suggesting that 1D/0D devices architecture may be successfully tuned for photodetector and other optoelectronic applications [58].

For instance, similar results were reported recently by Li et al. [59] showing that a QD decorated NWs demonstrated about 10 times higher photocurrent and responsivity than the bare NWs. The schematic illustration of a ZnO QD decorated Zn2SnO4 (ZTO) NW photodetector on a SiO2/Si substrate is shown in the inset of Fig. 5I. The spectral response of the two photodetector layouts was observed in the ultraviolet region, while almost zero response was observed in the visible and near-infrared region. Furthermore, 11–8 times higher responsivity of ZnO QD decorated ZTO NW was observed over the pristine ZTO NW at a wavelength irradiation of 305 and 300nm, respectively [59]. This distinct performance improvement was attributed to the energy band of the two materials, which allow a more efficient separation of electron-hole pairs at the interfaces between ZnO QDs and Zn2SnO4 nanowires (Fig. 5H). Similarly to the CQDs/Si NW devices, the built-in electric field induced by the fabricated heterojunction nanostructure allows the photogenerated holes to migrate to the ZnO, while the photogenerated electrons stay in ZTO, resulting in an increased device photoconductive gain (Fig. 5H) [59]. Fig. 5J shows the dynamic photocurrent response of these two photodetector configurations under an illumination wavelength of 300nmt with an intensity of 0.67μWcm−2 and a bias of 1V. Notably, the photocurrent of the QD-decorated devices is 8 times higher than that of the device without QDs, while both configurations demonstrated excellent reproducibility and stability over seven on/off cycles [59].

A key benefit of these nanomaterials is that the photocarrier dynamics in semiconductor NWs is strongly affected by the carrier trapping and/or scattering at surface localized energy states over that of the bulk energy states. This is particularly emphasized due to the large surface-to-volume ratio [2,60]. As a result, some key photodetection properties are size dependent within the appropriate length-scale. For instance, the size dependency of the photodetection in Ohmic Ge NW photoconductors was investigated by Kim et al. [61]. Fig. 6 A shows a conical Ge NW with each end of the NW with a diameter of 30 and 500nm is magnified in the insets. Multiterminal contacts were fabricated on these conical Ge NW with Ti/Au Ohmic metalizations (Fig. 6B). The photodetectivity (σ ph) response of Ohmic Ge NW devices with diameters of 50, 60, 150, and 290nm at a bias of 0.5V was investigated (Fig. 6C). It was observed that decreasing the diameter from 290 to 50nm enhanced the photodetectivity by more than two orders of magnitude independently of the illumination power (Fig. 6C) [61].

2D materials are an emerging class of nanostructured low-dimensional materials with a great potential in fabricating the next generation of miniaturized electronics and optoelectronics devices [62,63]. An example is graphene which has attracted substantial attention and led to extensive study in physics, materials, nano-engineering, and optoelectronic applications due to its extraordinary electrical properties such as high carrier mobility, broad absorption spectrum, and fast response time [50]. However, a lack of a bandgap hinders its potential for electronic device applications leading to a great motivation in exploring other 2D layered materials. Among them, transition metal dichalcogenides (TMDs) such as MoS2 and WS2 have received considerable attention due to their exceptional properties including a direct band gap in the visible range, large absorption coefficient, large exciton binding energy, and sensitivity to interlayer interactions [64–66].

Bulk MoS2 is an indirect-gap semiconductor with a bandgap of 1.2eV, whereas single-layer MoS2 is a direct-gap semiconductor with a bandgap of 1.8eV, as a result of quantum confinement [67,68]. In fact, the bandgap of MoS2 can be tuned by varying the number of layers in the crystal [67]. Benefiting from direct band gap of monolayer MoS2 phototransistor, Lopez et al. [69] demonstrated an ultrasensitive monolayer MoS2 photodetector (Fig. 7 A and B) with a photoresponsivity reaching 880AW−1, which exhibited a 100,000-fold improvement over previous reports for monolayer MoS2 phototransistors [70]. In order to fabricate the device, a scotch-tape-based micromechanical cleavage method was used to exfoliate monolayer MoS2 on a doped silicon substrate with 270nm thick layer of SiO2 on the top (Fig. 7A and C). Then, 90nm thick gold electrodes were deposited on top using electron-beam lithography and metal evaporation techniques (Fig. 7A and C). A focused laser beam with a wavelength of 561nm and an illumination power of 15μW was used to investigate the time-dependent photoresponse to laser excitation (Fig. 7A) [69].

For the wavelengths above 680nm, the photoresponsivity is negligible (Fig. 7E), which corresponds to the bandgap of monolayer MoS2 (photon energy of ca. 1.8eV) [67]. Decreasing the excitation wavelength down to 680nm resulted in the excitation of electrons from the VB to the CB and the generation of photocurrent (Fig. 7E). Fig. 7E inset illustrated the electrical characterization of the fabricated device under different illumination intensities in the 150pW–15μW range with a green laser with a wavelength of 561nm. For the small bias voltages (<1V), the drain current-bias voltage (I ds – V ds) curves were linear and symmetric indicating an ohmic-like contact. Under the illumination, the drain current (I illuminated
−I dark) was increased by several orders of magnitude due to the increase in carrier drift velocity and related reduction of the carrier transit time.

The observed behavior of this photodetector can be explained by a simple energy band diagram presented in Fig. 7D [69]. In the dark condition and zero bias (equilibrium state), the device is characterized by small Schottky barriers at the contacts. Illuminating the device with the wavelength of 680nm and in its OFF state (V g, V t), results in light absorption and electron-hole pairs generation, which can be extracted by applying a drain-source bias. In this condition, the photocurrent increases linearly with increasing the applied bias (V ds) due to the reduction of the carrier transit time. In the ON state (V g, V t), in addition to the photogenerated current, thermionic and tunneling currents also contribute to the device currents [69]. Increasing the gate voltage lowers the barriers at the contacts, resulting in an increased photoresponse. Operating the device in the OFF state brings the advantage of a reduced dark current.

In another approach, Tan et al. [71] fabricated an ultrathin 2D photodetector (with 2nm thickness) using graphene as a 2D electrode and monolayer WS2 as the active semiconductor. The fabrication process has been summarized in Fig. 7F. Initially, a chemical vapor deposition (CVD) method was used to grow graphene films and WS2 domains which were then transferred onto a chip (Si/SiO2 300nm) with predeposited Cr/Au bond pads (Fig. 7F). The electron-beam lithography was used to pattern the graphene which was later etched selectively by oxygen plasma into source and drain electrodes with a gap of certain width in between. A WS2/poly(methyl methacrylate) (PMMA) film was subsequently transferred onto the chip consisting the metal bond pads and graphene electrodes [71].

To characterize the performance of the fabricated photodetectors, the effect of channel lengths (200nm and 5μm) as well as number of layers (monolayer versus bilayer) on the device photoresponsivity was investigated (Fig. 7G). As mentioned before, the number of layers of WS2 has a large influence on the optical properties of the device including dark current [62]. In fact, the difference between the dark current in monolayer and bilayer WS2 is clear, with 10−8 A of bilayer WS2, which is two orders of magnitude larger than 10−10 A of monolayer device, as expected from Ohm’s law [71]. Fig. 7G shows the calculated responsivity of the four types of devices as a function to V ds, under illumination with light density of 3.8×106 mWcm−2. Increasing the applied voltage from 0 to 5V increased the photoresponsivity in all four types of devices, with the largest measured value to be over 3.5AW−1 for a bilayer WS2 device and 2.5AW−1 for monolayer device with short channel length of 200nm. The high performance of the device is mainly attributed to the modulation of the Schottky barrier between graphene and WS2 under an applied gate and increased illumination density [71].

Analogous to graphite and graphene, black phosphorus is a stack of phosphorene monolayers, bound together by van der Waals interactions which has recently received great attention for optoelectronic application [72]. Bulk black phosphorus shows a ~0.3eV direct bandgap which increases with decreasing thickness, and is predicted to have a direct bandgap (>1eV) in its monolayer form [72,73]. Using a CVD method, Deng et al. [74] fabricated an electrically tunable black phosphorus-monolayer MoS2 device on a 285nm SiO2/p+ doped Si substrate. The schematics of the device structure and the electrical connections are presented in Fig. 8 A. Using adhesive tape, black phosphorus was mechanically exfoliated from bulk material onto a SiO2 substrate on which CVD monolayer MoS2 had been synthesized. The thicknesses of the few-layer black phosphorus flake and monolayer MoS2 were ~11 and ~0.9nm, respectively [74].

To further understand the photoresponse process, photocurrent mapping was performed to determine the spatial distribution of the photocurrent generation (Fig. 8B) [74]. As seen in Fig. 8B and C, the current (which was measured at zero bias) is generated throughout the junction region indicating that charge separation is mainly happening in the heterojunction region. However, the photoresponse across the junction is not uniform (Fig. 8C), which could be attributed to the nonuniform contacts between the two flakes [74]. Moreover, the part of the junction region that near the metal contact on the MoS2 exhibits stronger photocurrent, which could be related to the lateral sheet resistance of MoS2 and black phosphorus [74].

Fig. 8D shows the gate tunable I-V characteristics of the p-n diode, and the inset (i) shows the I-V curves on a semilog plot. The current-rectifying characteristics can be modulated by the back-gate voltage, shown in the inset (ii) of Fig. 8D. The rectification ratio (which is defined as the ratio of the forward/reverse current) increased by decreasing the back-gate voltage from 2×102 of 50V to 105 of −30V. as the back-gate voltage decreases. In addition, the ideal factor of the p-n diode increases as the back-gate voltage increases and achieve a minimum value of 2.7 with a back-gate voltage of −30V. These strong current-rectifying characteristics could be attributed to the formation of van der Waals p-n heterojunction between p-type black phosphorus and n-type MoS2 [74].

3D nanostructured materials have attracted considerable research interest due to their large specific surface area [75–77]. As the ultimate performance of nanostructured materials is strongly relied on the sizes, shapes, dimensionality, and morphologies [78,79], it is of great interest to synthesize 3D nanostructured materials with a controlled nanostructure and morphology. Among different 3D nanostructures, 3D branched NW heterostructures offer attractive potential applications in sensing, photocatalysis, and optoelectronics arising from their enhanced surface area and function [80,81]. Using a cost-effective solution-phase process, Sun et al. [82] reported a low-cost, wafer-scale synthesis of ZnO/Si branched NW heterostructures and their application as high sensitivity photodetectors. Fig. 9 A shows the as-grown ZnO NW/Si NW heterostructures with hexagonal ZnO NWs (diameter around 30nm) on the Si NW core forming highly ordered 3D branched NW heterostructures. To fabricate the device, the heterostructure was embedded into an insulating polymer, followed by reactive ion etching (RIE). Then, a transparent contact was deposited on the top using indium tin oxide (ITO) and a Ti/Au probing pad (Fig. 9B). Fig. 9C shows the dark and measurements at room temperature and under xenon lamp illumination. Both dark and photocurrent voltage characteristics were rectifying with the best ON/OFF ratio achieved at −1V was about 250. The device demonstrated a maximum quantum efficiency was 2.20% and a responsivity as high as 12.8mA/W at around 900nm [82].

In a similar approach, a ZnO/ZnS type-II heterojunction core/shell nanowire array was fabricated as a high-performance broad band UV/visible photodetector [83]. Fig. 9D represents the top view of field emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM) image of fabricated ZnO/ZnS core/shell nanowire device on ITO coated glass substrate. A high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) image of a typical single ZnO/ZnS core/shell nanowire is displayed in Fig. 9E, where two different lattice fringes corresponding to the zinc-blend ZnS shell and wurtzite ZnO core are separated by an abrupt interface. The device can detect photons with energies significantly smaller (2.2eV) than the band gap of ZnO (3.2eV) and ZnS (3.7eV). This unique performance is attributed to spatially indirect type-II transition facilitated by the abrupt interface between the ZnO core and ZnS shell [83].

A typical I-V measurement of the fabricated device under a UV (385nm) excitation source is displayed in Fig. 9F. It can be seen that the photocurrent increases with an increase in the UV illumination density which is mainly attributed to increment in the of electron-hole pairs generation rate at higher illumination density [83]. As E g,ZnS (3.7eV) [84] > E 385nm (3.22eV)≈ E g,ZnO (3.3eV) [4], the excitation wavelength used only matches ZnO optical band gap, and it is reasonable to assume that the photocurrent contribution from the electron-hole pairs predominantly arise from photoabsorption taking place in the ZnO core in a core/shell nanowire.

Low-dimensional nanostructured materials, such as nanowires and nanoparticles, have been demonstrated to show high photosensitivity, as their large surface-to-volume ratio can enhance the light-scattering effect and light harvesting. However, a precise synthetic fabrication process such as a combination of lithography, etching, and deposition is required to achieve the desired high performance, while these fabrication methods are complicated, time consuming, and uneconomic. Therefore, it is of great interest to develop new materials for high-performance photodetectors using easy fabrication method.

Recently, Zhang et al. [85] fabricated a novel photoreactor based in unique CH3NH3PbI3 perovskite films through a simple and cost-effective solution method. The obtained CH3NH3PbI3 films were composed of continuous micrometer-sized islands networks, exhibiting wide optical absorption over a broad range of wavelength [85]. The surface topography and cross-section image of the island-structured CH3NH3PbI3 film on a substrate is shown in Fig. 9G and H illustrating a 375-nm thick layer of perovskite film. The photodetection performance of the fabricated device was significantly enhanced compared to that of the conventional compact CH3NH3PbI3 devices, which is mainly attributed to the existence of islands network [85]. The transient photoresponse behavior of the devices was investigated with the illumination switched on and off at different bias voltages (5, 15, 30, and 60V), as shown in Fig. 9I [85]. There is nearly no significant variation in the signal amplitude during the on/off cycles, indicating a good reversibility and stability of the device. In addition to that, the fabricated devices showed a photo to dark current ratio of ca. 104 at an illumination density of 6.59mWcm−2 which is significantly higher than that of previously reported devices [86,87]. This high performance is mainly attributed to the unique micrometer-sized island networks morphology of CH3NH3PbI3 film, resulting in the presence of neighboring islands and the “hopping-like” charge transfer [88].

A similar results were reported by Nasiri et al. [4] as they demonstrated ultraporous 3D ultraporous nanoparticle networks obtained by thermophoretic-driven deposition of ZnO aerosols on the glass substrates featuring a set of interdigitated electrodes. Fig. 10 A shows exemplary film obtained by deposition of the hot ZnO nanoparticle with an average particle diameter of 20nm (Fig. 10B) resulting in an ultrahigh film porosity of ca. 98%, high purity, visible light transparency and ca. 80% UV light abortion [8]. Upon deposition, thermally induced annealing of the nanoparticle grain boundaries was performed at annealing temperature of 250°C–400°C to investigate the optimal annealing temperature for the fabricated device. As presented in Fig. 10C, the maximal responsivity peaked at ≈7AW−1 for the 300°C annealed device under a light illumination with wavelength of 370nm, which matches well the bandgap of ZnO (3.37eV). The optimized device featured ultralow dark current of 22pA, high photocurrent of 205μA, and I UV/I dark ratio of 9.3×106 at a light intensity of 86μWcm−2 and bias of 1V (Fig. 10D). This optimal performance was attributed to thermal-induced formation of an electron-depleted open-neck intergrain morphology resulted in very low pA dark currents and high photocurrents [8].

Despite significant progress in fabricating ultrahigh-performance semiconductor-based photodetectors, the steady-state response dynamics of the fabricated devices was in the range of 150–300s due to the adsorption and desorption of O2 trapping states [4,5,7,8]. To overcome this bottleneck, a 3D network of nanoscale heterojunctions (3DNH) was introduced by Nasiri et al. [9] using a 98% porous film of n-type ZnO nanoparticles coated with p-type NiO nano-clusters (Fig. 10E and F). The built-in electric fields between the nanoscale p- and n-type domains enable the rapid separation of the photogenerated electron-hole pairs decreasing the photocurrent rise and decay times by 26- and 20-fold, respectively (Fig. 10H) [9]. In addition to that, the fabricated devices exhibited a significantly higher photodetection performance after NiO cluster deposition resulted in the highest photocurrent of 345μA (Fig. 10G) and the lowest dark current of 15pA (at 1V) for the device with the longest NiO sputtering time. This resulted in a I UV/I dark ratios exceeding 107 at a bias of 1V and a light density of 80μWcm−2 which is the highest ratio so far reported for UV photodetectors under a similar illumination and applied bias [4,7,88,89].

In another study, they proposed a 3D nanoscale architecture, composed of three ultraporous functional layers (ZnO, SiO2, and TiO2) (Fig. 11 A) for band-selective UV photodetectors that features excellent tenability and light detection performance [5]. A band-selective photoresponse is obtained by exploiting the transmittance window between the indirect bandgap of TiO2 [90] and the sharp edge of the direct bandgap of ZnO [5,91]. Increasing the TiO2 film thickness from 1.6 to 6.6μm narrowed the bandwidth of the spectral response (Fig. 11B) while preserving the peak response at a wavelength of 370nm, which is ideally located in the UVA range [5]. Using this novel architecture, the bandwidth of these photodetectors was tuned from 85 nm of the single ZnO layer to 29nm of the TiO2/SiO2/ZnO network (Fig. 11B). This resulted in a significant enhancement (a factor of 20) in I UVA/I UVB selectivity of the fabricated device, from 0.82 of the single ZnO layer to 16 of the TiO2/SiO2/ZnO network (Fig. 11C). This solid-state architecture can be implemented with other top-bottom layer compositions such as GaAs (1.4eV)–Si (1.1eV), and SnO2 (3.7eV)–ZnS (3.54eV) enabling the fabrication of portable band-selective photodetectors for the UV, visible, and infrared spectrums [5].

In this chapter, we have reported some of the latest achievements and understanding on the use of nanomaterials for the design of high-performance UV photodetectors. An introduction to the key figure of merit of photodetectors as well as to their application to the detection of UV light was presented. The benefits promised by the development of novel photodetector materials that can provide unique physical properties and innovative light transduction mechanism were discussed with the aim of providing directions for this highly researched topic. Typical photodetector device configurations were summarized emphasizing strengths and weakness of the various layouts. The most recent progress in the engineering and use of low-dimensional materials were reviewed. The unique achievements of 0D–3D nanomaterials were discussed highlighting the most successful material-morphology combinations. We believe that nanostructured materials with a number of intriguing and excellent photoelectric properties have established themselves as versatile building blocks for the design of the future general of UV photodetectors. These novel capabilities will allow the fabrication of innovative devices overcoming some of the current limitation in terms of power consumption, size, and functionalities.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Vertical self-alignment of SiGe nanolenses on Si(001)
Meeting Abstracts, No. 2, The Electrochemical Society
Nanoarchitechtonics of visible-blind ultraviolet photodetector materials: critical features and nano-microfabrication
